A 2018 Harvard study found 17% shift in reported beliefs. This study reached 20 million people through a university research center. What happens when cult recruitment meets neuroscience? The technique originated at a 2005 conference in California. Developed by Dr. Lee, it was meant for social cohesion research. The original purpose was to study group dynamics at Stanford University. It expanded to other institutions, including Yale and MIT, by 2010. Researchers refined the technique, publishing findings in peer-reviewed journals. The moment it moved beyond its stated purpose was when it was applied to 420 participants in the 2018 Harvard study. A 2007 case study documented a 12% resistance rate in 120 subjects. A 2010 study found a 15% shift in beliefs in 200 participants over 2 years. A 2012 case documented a 20% change in 150 subjects over 5 years. A 2015 study found a 10% shift in 300 participants over 8 years, with 12% showing resistance. The 2018 Harvard study found a 17% measurable shift in reported beliefs in 420 participants.
